title: Star Wars: Dark Forces
content:
{{Top|rwm|reb|italics=1}} {{MultipleIssues|citation|redlink}} {{VideoGame |image=[[File:Dark_Forces_Box_Cover.jpg]] |title=''Star Wars: Dark Forces'' |developer=[[LucasArts]] |publisher=LucasArts |release date=*[[February 15]], [[1995]] (PC)''[[Star Wars Year By Year: A Visual History, New Edition]]'' *[[June 12]], [[1995]] (Mac){{WebCite|url=https://publicrecords.copyright.gov/detailed-record/voyager_11768693|text=Star Wars--Dark forces.|work=the [[Wikipedia:United States Copyright Office|U.S. Copyright Office Public Records System]]|archivedate=20251009145217}} |closed= |upc= |engine=[[Jedi Engine]] |genre=[[Wikipedia:First-person shooter|First-person shooter]] |modes=[[Wikipedia:Single player|Single player]] |ratings=[[Wikipedia:ESRB|ESRB]]: Teen (T) |platforms=*[[Wikipedia:Personal computer|Personal computer]] *[[Wikipedia:Mac (computer)|Mac]] *[[Wikipedia:PlayStation|PlayStation]] *[[Wikipedia:PlayStation 3|PlayStation 3]] *[[Wikipedia:PlayStation Portable|PlayStation Portable]] |series= |basegame= |expansions= |director= |producer= |designer= |programmer= |artist= |writer= |composer=[[Clint Bajakian]] |timeline=[[0 BBY/Legends|0 BBY]]–[[1 ABY/Legends|1 ABY]]''[[The New Essential Chronology]]'' |prev= |next=''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight: Dark Forces II]]'' }} '''''Star Wars: Dark Forces''''' is a first-person shooter computer game released on [[February 15]], [[1995]], by [[LucasArts]]. The game introduces the character of [[Kyle Katarn]], a former Imperial [[Stormtrooper/Legends|stormtrooper]] and agent, now a mercenary for hire in the service of the [[Alliance to Restore the Republic/Legends|Rebel Alliance]], and uses the 3D [[Jedi Engine]]. The story takes place both just before and mostly after the events of [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'': Episode IV ''A New Hope'']].''[[Dark Forces Official Player's Guide]]'' A [[Star Wars: Dark Forces Remaster|remaster]] of the game was announced to be under development for PC, [[Wikipedia:Xbox Series X and Series S|Xbox Series X/S]], [[Wikipedia:Playstation 5|PlayStation 5]], and [[Wikipedia:Nintendo Switch|Nintendo Switch]] on [[August 23]], [[2023]] by [[Nightdive Studios]]. It would feature cutscenes redone in higher quality graphics and remastered content.{{OfficialYouTube|channel=@IGN|name=IGN|link=Wikipedia:IGN|video=-AKxEREJqgU|text=Star Wars: Dark Forces Remastered – Official Announcement Trailer|date=2023-08-23|quote=Get your first look at the remaster of the classic 1995 Star Wars first-person shooter Dark Forces, which is being developed by Nightdive Studios, the same team responsible for the recent remasters of Quake 2 and System Shock. Star Wars: Dark Forces Remastered is in development for all major platforms (PC, Xbox, PlayStation, and Nintendo Switch).}} The remaster was released [[February 28]], [[2024]].{{SW|url=news/dark-forces-remaster|text=''Dark Forces'' ''Remaster'' Comes to Consoles and PC}}
Meanwhile, a continually developed community made port entitled The Force Engine reached 1.0 on December 20, 2022.[https://theforceengine.github.io The Force Engine (TFE)] ==Opening crawl== {{OpeningCrawl |name=DARK FORCES |text=The New Order of the Empire stretches its evil clutches across the galaxy, consuming planets with devastating results. Through many struggles, the Rebel Alliance has learned of a new Imperial battle station, the DEATH STAR, with enough power to destroy an entire planet. Unable to acquire the plans to the deadly space station, the Rebels have employed the skills of Kyle Katarn. Known to most as a mercenary for hire, Katarn is a rogue figure who has a partial alliance with the Rebels. Armed only with a blaster pistol and an intimate knowledge of Imperial methods, Kyle prepares to infiltrate the Imperial base where the plans are kept…}} ==Plot summary== [[File:BeardlessKyleKatarn-PreTalay-SWDF.png|thumb|left|250px|Introducing [[Kyle Katarn]].]] [[Kyle Katarn]] infiltrates an Imperial base on the planet [[Danuta]], in order to steal the [[Death Star plans/Legends|Death Star plans]]. The [[Mission to Danuta]] results in Rebel possession of the plans that would later be given to [[Princess/Legends|Princess]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia]] and the destruction of the [[DS-1 Orbital Battle Station/Legends|Death Star]]. After the [[Battle of Yavin/Legends|Battle of Yavin]], Kyle is again contacted by [[Mon Mothma/Legends|Mon Mothma]] to investigate an Imperial assault on the [[Tak Base]] of [[Talay/Legends|Talay]], using a type of Imperial trooper never encountered previously. After the [[attack on Tak Base]] Kyle infiltrates the ruins of the base and discovers a prototype dark trooper weapon. The initials "M.R." are discovered on it, which are found to stand for [[Moff/Legends|Moff]] [[Rebus (Galactic Empire)|Rebus]], an infamous Imperial weapons engineer. This investigation reveals the [[Dark Trooper/Legends|dark trooper]] project, led by [[General/Legends|General]] [[Rom Mohc]], and this leads Katarn to [[Anoat/Legends|Anoat]], where he finds the Moff hiding in the [[Dianoga]]-filled sewage system of [[Anoat City]]. During interrogation, Moff Rebus reveals the location of a testing facility for the mineral [[Phrik/Legends|phrik]], used in the construction of dark troopers, on the planet [[Fest/Legends|Fest]]. After obtaining a sample of this mineral, Kyle is led to the [[Gromas system/Legends|Gromas system]], where phrik is mined and the [[Phase I Dark Trooper|phase-I dark troopers]] are manufactured. After the destruction of this facility, [[Crix Madine/Legends|Crix Madine]] is found to have been captured by the Empire and sent to an Imperial detention center on [[Orinackra]]. Once Kyle has rescued Madine, he provides Kyle with critical information regarding the dark trooper project. Kyle travels to the [[Ramsees Hed]] docking port on [[Cal-Seti/Legends|Cal-Seti]], which is used for Imperial runs to the frozen planet of [[Anteevy/Legends|Anteevy]], where the [[Phase II Dark Trooper|second phase]] of dark trooper construction was completed. After smuggling himself aboard an Imperial ship, Kyle reaches Anteevy and destroys the [[Ice Station Beta/Legends|Ice Station Beta]] facility. He is afterwards led to [[Nar Shaddaa/Legends|Nar Shaddaa]], where [[Mission to Nar Shaddaa (Dark Trooper campaign)|he obtains]] a [[Nava card]]. At this point in the scenario, the Empire has placed a large bounty on his head, and as he pulls out of Nar Shaddaa, the ''[[Moldy Crow/Legends|Moldy Crow]]'' is captured by [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]] in the [[Star Jewel/Legends|''Star Jewel'']]. [[File:Ramsees_Hed.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Kyle Katarn in [[Ramsees Hed]].]] Here, Kyle is stripped of his weapons and has to [[Mission to Nar Shaddaa (Dark Trooper campaign)|fight hand-to-hand]] with a [[Kell dragon/Legends|kell dragon]]. After obtaining his weapons, he and Jan Ors [[Mission to Coruscant (Kyle Katarn)|escape]] to [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]], the only place where Kyle can insert the Nava Card into a decoder to reveal data chips used for additional information. On his way to his ship, Kyle finds the Crow missing and [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]] (hired by Mohc) waiting for him. He defeats Fett after a hard fight and then travels to the Imperial [[Fuel Station Ergo/Legends|Fuel Station Ergo]], where he sneaks onto the [[Executor/Legends|''Executor'']]. From here, he smuggles himself aboard the ''[[Arc Hammer]]'', where the [[Phase-III Dark Trooper|third and final phase]] of the dark troopers is being overseen. Finally, Kyle faces [[Rom Mohc]], spearhead of the operation (who is using the only phase-III dark trooper exoskeleton in existence), and defeats him. Kyle proceeds to blow up the ''Arc Hammer'' and escape. Watching from the [[Executor/Legends|''Executor'']], [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]] comments that this "is an unfortunate setback" and somewhat prophetically notes that "the Force is strong with [[Star Wars: Jedi Knight: Dark Forces II|Katarn]]." For his actions and bravery in the face of defeat, Mon Mothma awards Kyle the [[Star of Alderaan/Legends|Star of Alderaan]]. ==Gameplay== The missions follow a storyline, sometimes interrupted by videos to progress the tale. Each mission has its own briefing and objective. The levels were designed to represent actual bases, mines, facilities, and other known places from the ''Star Wars'' universe, like [[Star Destroyer/Legends|Star Destroyer]] interiors, [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba's]] ship, [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]], etc. ===Levels=== *[[Mission to Danuta|The Death Star Plans - Operation Skyhook: Part II]] *[[Mission to Talay|Talay: Tak Base, After The Massacre]] *[[Mission to Anoat|Anoat City - The Subterranean Hideout]] *[[Mission to Fest (Galactic Civil War)|The Planet Fest: Imperial Weapons Research Facility]] *[[Mission to Gromas|Gromas Mines - The Blood Moon]] *[[Mission to Orinackra|Imperial Detention Center, Orinackra: Crix Madine's Fate]] *[[Mission to Cal-Seti|Ramsees Hed Docking Port, Cal-Seti - Deadly Cargo]] *[[Mission to Anteevy|Robotics Construction Facility, Anteevy - Ice Station Beta]] *[[Mission to Nar Shaddaa (Dark Trooper campaign)|Nar Shaddaa, the Vertical City: The Death Mark]] *[[Mission to Nar Shaddaa (Dark Trooper campaign)#Captured by Jabba the Hutt|Jabba the Hutt's ship: Jabba's Revenge]] *[[Mission to Coruscant (Kyle Katarn)|Imperial City, Coruscant - The Imperial Mask]] *[[Mission to Fuel Station Ergo|Imperial Fuel Station, Ergo - Smuggler's Hijack]] *[[Mission to the Executor|The Executor - The Stowaway]] *[[Mission to the Arc Hammer|The Arc Hammer - The Dark Awakening]] ==Development== {{Quote|Unlike other 3-D games, ''Dark Forces'' had to seem real because ''Star Wars'' seems real to so many people. This would be the first time anyone could aftually walk around in a ''Star Wars'' universe. It had to have the same rich look and feel and sound and detail of the movies, plus compelling gameplay.|[[Daron Stinnett]], project leader|}} ''Dark Forces'' started when [[Daron Stinnett]] was hired by LucasArts in July, [[1993]]. Initially Stinnett thought about a flight simulator but turned towards a [[Wikipedia:First-person shooter|first-person shooter]], impressed by the revolutionary [[Wikipedia:Wolfenstein 3D|Wolfenstein 3D]]. As the genre was still an emerging trend, the team he assembled was based simply on talent than on experience. Along with [[Ray Gresko]] and [[Winston Wolff]], he started in late August to develop the [[Jedi Engine]], the 3d engine of the game. Stinnett also hired [[Wikipedia:University of California, Berkeley|University of California, Berkeley]] architectural undergrads [[Ingar Shu]] and [[Matthew Tateishi|Matt Tateishi]] as level designers. The core of the team was ten young individuals, occasionally rising to twenty. Initially conceived as reenacting the adventures of [[Luke Skywalker]] in [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|Star Wars: Episode IV ''A New Hope'']], it was considered too limited and brief; an original character was then conceived, who would become [[Kyle Katarn]], who followed his own adventures, albeit within the limits of the established canon. [[Justin Chin]], who joined the team in December of that year, thought that the 3-d engine could tell new stories, and described the original idea about "a special force of Stormtroopers" to Gresko and Stinnett. The first design document described the characters, and a layout of 32 missions (later reduced to 14), each with a different objective, and set on a different planet, with its own look and feel. Chin considered ''Dark Forces'' an adventure packed with physical action sequences which, except ''Star Wars'', should have elements from famous action films. Chin provided text notes and concept artwork to the level designers, who worked on their familiar [[Wikipedia:AutoCAD|AutoCAD]] software. Five individuals worked for the textures, sceneries and surface details, producing thousands of pieces and animations, which were organised by [[Aaron Muszalski]]. Technically, the design had to keep a balance between providing rich visual details that would give the familiar ''Star Wars'' ambience, but within the technical limitations of the game engine, in order to allow for speed and a fast action. What was at first an experimental project by an inexperienced team, it was when the game was about 60% complete when they started to figure things out and gained some confidence about the finished result and decided on some additions and changes. Some of the latest changes was the [[Wikipedia:Spawning (video games)#Enemy respawning|regeneration]] of enemy sprites, and the creation of a new engine for cutscenes, and the character of [[Jan Ors]] to advance the story. Other changes were the addition of extra lives among the powerups, and "restart points" after each player's death, instead of returning to the level's beginning. [[Brett Tosti]] and his playtester team arranged the strategic position of powerups, enemies, and their A.I., in order to improve challenge and playability. A character named [[Ruu San]] was meant to appear in the game as an antagonist. However, she did not appear in the final release of the game.{{GalaxyCite|1|Game Room|Game Room: Revving Up the 'Jedi Engine'}} In early production, the player's ship was named simply ''Crow'' and was intended to be a [[Baudo-class star yacht|''Baudo''-class star yacht]]. Also, it was not a 3-D polygonal object, but a two-dimensional bitmap sprite rendered from different view angles.{{BookCite|author=Mizell, Leslie|container=Dark Forces: The Jedi Return... and Face a New Type of Stormtrooper|title=[[Wikipedia:PC Gamer|PC Gamer]]|number=Vol.1 No.5|publisher=[[Wikipedia:Future plc|Future plc]]|date=[[October]] [[1994]]|link=https://web.archive.org/web/20240926103148/https://df-21.net/articles/scans/?magazine=pc-gamer-5}} The first mission, that would involve the protagonist stealing the [[Death Star plans/Legends|Death Star plans]], was to [[Mission on the Avenger|take place]] on the [[Imperial-class Star Destroyer/Legends|''Imperial''-class Star Destroyer]] [[Avenger/Legends|''Avenger'']]. That level was presented as an [[Star Wars: Dark Forces Consumer Electronics Show demo|early demo]] but eventually was cut during development due to concerns of it being too difficult for new players. The level was replaced with [[Mission to Danuta|another]] set on a [[Danuta research facility|secret base]] in the released game and aspects of the level were recycled into other parts of the game. The more than 300 sound effects included signature ''Star Wars'' effects provided from [[Skywalker Sound]] which were used verbatim (such as for a [[TIE/LN_starfighter|TIE fighter]]) or tweaked for newly appearing elements (such as original weapons); others were from professional sound libraries, or from [[Clint Bajakian]]'s own collection. Processed in high resolution, they eventually had to be carefully sampled down to 8-bit mono sound, necessary for the gameplay. Voice roles were recorded in a [[Wikipedia:Los Angeles|Los Angeles]] studio, and the digital recordings were brought to Bajakian's studio. Bajakian also worked on the soundtrack. As the game combined [[Original trilogy|classical]] with [[Star Wars Legends|original]] elements, his intention was to emulate the music from the movies, but with variety and uniqueness. The appearances of [[Anakin Skywalker|Darth Vader]] in the cutscenes corresponded to score from the movies, but new characters had their own original theme, compatible with the established sound by [[John Williams]]. [[Rom Mohc]]'s theme, that accompanies the opening credits, has dark and foreboding, contrasted by Kyle Katarn's theme, which is closely related, but heroic and upbeat. Familiar motifs such as [[The Imperial March]], the [[Imperial motif]] or the [[Rebel Fanfare]] are incorporated in the soundtracks of some missions. According to the [[iMUSE]] technique, the in-game music follows the action of the gameplay, seamlessly alternating between two moods: peaceful ''stalk music'' for the portions of exploration and solo travel, and frenetic ''fight music'' when the player is under attack or surrounded by enemies. ''Doom'' had shared textures for all levels, one solid objective (which was simply to move from entry to exit), and maps which were designed often very abstractly, with the architecture often somewhat unrealistic. The levels of ''Dark Forces'' each had their own unique texture sets that were rarely reused, their own briefings and objectives which often varied, and maps which were designed to represent actual bases, mines, facilities, and other known places from the ''Star Wars'' universe, like [[Star Destroyer/Legends|Star Destroyer]] interiors, [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba's]] ship, [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]], etc. Lead artist and author [[Justin Chin]] stated that weapons like the [[Bryar pistol/Legends|Bryar pistol]] and [[Packered mortar gun]] were named after his personal favorites, such as composer Gavin Bryars and 1950s Packard automobiles, respectively.Email from Chin recorded in ''The Dark Forces FAQList'' All ''Dark Forces'' cheat codes start with LA- (LucasArts), paralleling the cheat codes of [[Wikipedia:id Software|id Software]] titles, which always began with ID-. ==Release and reception== A [[Star Wars: Dark Forces Consumer Electronics Show demo|playable demo]] was available at the [[Wikipedia:Consumer Electronics Show|Consumer Electronics Show]] featuring a playable prototype of the game's early first level, [[Mission on the Avenger|taking place]] on the [[Avenger/Legends|''Avenger'']]. Screenshots from that level was featured in promotional material even after that level was removed.{{SW|url=news/dark-forces-remaster|text=''Dark Forces'' ''Remaster'' Comes to Consoles and PC|date=2024-02-28}}''[[Star Wars: Dark Forces Remaster]]'' The first level was replaced with [[Mission to Danuta|another]] set on a [[Danuta research facility|secret base]] in the released game. The new first mission was also released independently as a demo of the game. The intro of the demo is different from the published product in that it contains a Rambo-ish cutscene showing Kyle preparing his gear for the mission. This was omitted from the final game. The reason for this is not clear since the files of that sequence already exist in the CD. However, it's possible to reinsert the missing scene into the game.{{WebCite|url=http://df-21.net/downloads/misc/cutscene.zip|text=DF-21.net|archivedate=20060504223831}} The game was released on [[February 15]], [[1995]]''[[Star Wars Year By Year: A Visual History, New Edition]]'' It was originally published with a [[Dark Forces Manual: Coded Transmissions|manual]] which contained additional backstory text. On April 29, 2015, the game was released on [[PlayStation Network]], and made available to play on both [[Wikipedia:PlayStation 3|PlayStation 3]] and [[Wikipedia:PlayStation Portable|PlayStation Portable]]. ==Legacy== The game was followed by novelizations and a sequel, ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight: Dark Forces II]]''. ''Jedi Knight'' spawned an entire series of games which includes the expansion ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight: Mysteries of the Sith]]'', [[Star Wars: Jedi Knight II: Jedi Outcast|''Jedi Knight II: Jedi Outcast'']] and ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight: Jedi Academy]]''. This series, with the exception of ''Jedi Academy'', focuses on the continuing exploits of [[Kyle Katarn]], many of which take place after the events of [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi'']]. Many of the key data files in ''Dark Forces'' were actually plain-text files, allowing fans to decipher the formats and write tools to edit them. These have been used to create a wide variety of new levels for the game.{{WebCite|url=http://df-21.net/downloads|text=DF-21.net|archivedate=20200215001329}} Many of the same fans have gone on to create tools and editors for the later games in the series; and at least two, Yves Borckmans and Don Sielke, joined LucasArts to work on ''Dark Forces II''. In 2002, a team of fans began the Dark Forces Mod project, planning to remake the game utilizing the ''Quake III'' engine used in the latest sequel, ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight II: Jedi Outcast]]'' (later adapted to ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight: Jedi Academy]]'' when it was released). Later, they announced that they would be ending the project,{{WebCite|url=http://darkforces.jediknight.net/index2.shtml|text=Dark Forces: A MOD for Jedi Academy|archivedate=20070407194606}} and posted a final release containing full remakes of the first six levels of the game. However, an effort to remake the remaining levels was still underway as of January 2009.{{Blogspot|subdomain=dfmod|url=2009/01/school-started-this-week.html|name=DF Mod Blogspot|text=School Started This Week}} ==Media== {{Mediacat|from=1|imagecat=Images from Star Wars: Dark Forces|soundcat=Audio files from Star Wars: Dark Forces}} ===Cover gallery=== File:Dark_Forces_Box_Cover.jpg|Cover File:DarkForcesPSX.jpg|Playstation cover ==Credits== {{IncompleteCredits}} {{Credits |cast= *[[Nick Jameson]]: [[Kyle Katarn]] *[[Jack Angel]]: [[Rom Mohc]] *[[Denny Delk]]: [[Stormtrooper/Legends|Stormtrooper]], [[Toka]], Narrator *[[Julie Eccles]]: [[Jan Ors]] *[[Scott Lawrence]]: [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]] *[[Peggy Roberts-Hope]]: [[Mon Mothma/Legends|Mon Mothma]] |crew= *Project Leader - [[Daron Stinnett]] *Lead Artist - [[Justin Chin]] *3D Engine - [[Ray Gresko]] *Story - Justin Chin *Programming - Daron Stinnett, Ray Gresko and [[Winston Wolff]] *Game Design - The Dark Forces Team with Special Thanks to Justin Chin *Level Design - [[Ingar Shu]], [[Jim Current]], [[Matthew Tateishi]] and [[Brett Tosti]] *Tools - Winston Wolff *Macintosh version - [[Aaron Giles]] *Mac Programming Assistance - [[Eric Johnson (programmer)|Eric Johnson]], [[Brad Post]] and the Apple/Motorola Power PC Games Kitchen Staff *Texture Placement - [[Aaron Muszalski]] and [[Chris Ross]] *Texture Artwork - [[Chris Hockabout]], [[Patrick Sirk]] and [[Tom Payne]] *3D Artwork - [[Ralph Gerth IV]], [[Scott Baker]], [[David Vallone]], [[Ron Lussier]] and [[Bill Stoneham]] *2D Cutscene Artwork - Justin Chin, [[Jon Knoles]] and [[Paul Mica]] *Art Technician - Aaron Muszalski *Video Effects - [[Michael Levine]], [[C. Andrew Melson]] and [[Mark Christiansen]] *Animations - [[Leonard Robel]] and [[Gordon Baker]] *Additional Art Design - Paul Mica *iMUSE Interactive Music System - [[Michael Land]], [[Peter McConnell]], [[Michael McMahon]] and [[Justin Graham]] *Music and Orchestration - [[Clint Bajakian]] *Digital Sound Effects - Clint Bajakian *Voice Directing and Editing - [[Khris Brown]] *Voice Producer - [[Tamlynn Barra]] *Install Utility - [[Gary Brubaker]] *Landru System Developed By - [[Edward Kilham]] *Executive Producer - [[Steve Dauterman]] *Project Coordination - [[Dan Connors]] *Lead Testers - Brett Tosti (IBM) & [[Dana Fong]] (Mac) *Testers - [[Chris Snyder]], [[Chris Purvis]], [[Leyton Chew]], [[Reed Derleth]], [[Darren Johnson]], [[William W. Burns]], [[Al Chen]], [[John Hannon]], Dana Fong, [[Brian Kemp]] and [[Sean Matheis]] *Compatibility Testing - [[Doyle Gilstrap]] and [[Chip Hinnenberg]] *Quality Assurance Supervisor - [[Mark Cartwright]] *Product Marketing Manager - [[Barbara Gleason]] *Public Relations - [[Sue Seserman]] and [[Tom Sarris]] *Package Design - Jon Knoles, Justin Chin and [[Terri SooHoo]] *Package Cover Art (front) - Ron Lussier *Package Cover Art (back) - David Vallone *CD-ROM disc Art - David Vallone *Manual Written by - [[Jo Ashburn|Jo "Captain Tripps" Ashburn]], Mark Cartwright and Brett Tosti *Manual Design - Shepard Associates *Product Support - [[Mara Kaehn]] (Manager), [[Jason Deadrich]] (Supervisor), [[Tabitha Tosti]] & the Product Support Team *Key Accounts Manager - [[Meredith Cahill]] *Manufacturing Manager - [[Jason Horstman]] *International Manager - [[Lisa Star]] *Additional Programming - [[Mark Haigh-Hutchinson]] *Original Music Score - [[John Williams]] |specialthanks= *[[Wayne Cline]], [[Don Bies|Donald Bies]], [[Aric Wilmunder]], [[Eric Klein]], [[Lucy Autrey Wilson|Lucy Wilson]], [[Stacy Cheregotis|Stacy A. *[[Jeron/Legends|Jeron]] {{1stm}} *[[Galactic Empire/Legends|Galactic Empire]] **[[Imperial officer/Legends|Imperial officer]] ***[[General/Legends|General]] ***[[Moff/Legends|Moff]] **[[Imperial
